title: A Suggested Learning Path
subtitle: What to learn first, second, third — and what to ignore until later.
summary: A staged plan for new Vim users: what to learn in week 1, what to add in month 1, what to layer on after that. Helps avoid the firehose problem.

blocks.text: Vim has hundreds of commands. You don't need them all. Here's a staged path that gets you productive fast, then adds layers.
Get to the point where you can edit anything without panic.

rows: Move (use them — do NOT use arrows)

blocks.text: The biggest leap. Stop typing characters; start composing operators with motions.

Pick up as needed: registers beyond the unnamed, {key:g}{key:n}/{key:c}{key:g}{key:n} for refactoring loops, the bracket prefix family, the {key:g}/{key:z} prefixes, folding, the terminal, the undo tree.

blocks.title: The Anti-Pattern: Trying to Learn Everything
blocks.text:
The biggest reason people give up on Vim: they try to memorize the whole reference card before they can do anything. Don't. Learn 10 commands until they're muscle memory. Then 10 more. The grammar (operator+motion) makes the *next* 100 commands almost free.

If you're consciously thinking about which key to press, you're not yet at the point where Vim feels productive. Push through with a small set; the speed-up comes when actions become subconscious.
